segments.s078-g1: بزد is a past simple (3rd‑person singular) verb meaning “he struck/beat” and here governs two following objects. نای مهراب is a genitival phrase (noun + noun) “the ney of the mihrab” (mihrab = mosque niche/altar). و links the two coordinated verb phrases and introduces بربست کوس, where بربست is an archaic/periphrastic past verb meaning roughly “set up/sounded” and کوس is its object “war‑drum.” The whole clause describes two simultaneous soundings: the flute and the drum.
segments.s078-g2: بیاراست is a past (or perfective) verbal form meaning “(he/they) adorned” or “was adorned”; here it applies to لشکر (“the army/host”) as its object. چو is a simile particle meaning “like/as,” and چشم خروس is the comparison “the (bright) eye of the rooster.” In sum: the army was decked/adorned, compared to a rooster’s eye (a vivid, colorful image).
segments.s078-g3: ابا here functions as a poetic variant of the preposition با (“with”), introducing the accompanying figures; however this spelling/form is archaic or dialectal. ژنده‌پیلان is a compound descriptive noun “tattered elephants” (ژنده = ragged, پیل = elephant) and و connects رامشگران (“peacemakers” or those who pacify/comfort). The whole phrase lists companions or elements with which the scene is filled.
segments.s078-g4: زمین شد is a subject + past copula “the earth became.” بهشت is the predicate complement “paradise.” از کران تا کران is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning “from edge to edge” or “from one end to the other” (از … تا marking the extent). Together: “the earth became paradise from end to end.”
segments.s078-g5: ز بس is a poetic locution meaning “because of so many / on account of the abundance of.” گونه گون is a duplicated-stem adjective meaning “variegated / of many kinds” (here split into two words for metrical reasons). پرنیانی is an adjectival form “silken” and درفش is the noun “banner/standard,” so the phrase describes an abundance of many‑colored silk standards.
segments.s078-g6: This segment lists contrasting colors introduced by the exclamatory particle چه (“what/how” used to emphasize). The pattern “چه A و B و چه C و D” is rhetorical: “whether A and B or C and D.” Here the colors سرخ (red), سپید (white), زرد (yellow) and بنفش (purple) enumerate the banner hues.
segments.s078-g7: The opening چه is exclamatory and stresses the following sounds. آوای (the ezāfe‑less form used poetically) means “the sound/melody of,” so آوای نای و آوای چنگ lists two musical timbres: the ney (flute) and the chang (harp). The parallelism sets up a catalogue of sounds.
segments.s078-g8: خروشیدن is a deverbal infinitive/masdar meaning “the roaring/clamor” or “to roar,” here nominalized to name a sound. It takes بوق (“trumpet/horn”) as its source. و links this to آوای زنگ “the sound/ringing of the bell.” The clause names loud martial noises: trumpet blasts and bell‑ringing.
segments.s078-g89: بترسم is a first‑person present/imperfect verb with the prefixed ب‑, here meaning “I fear” or “I am afraid.” ز is the preposition “from/because of” that introduces the cause. آشوب is the noun “turmoil/tumult,” while بد is an adjective “bad/evil” that directly modifies the following plural noun گوهران (“essences/ones”); together آشوب بد گوهران = “the tumult of bad‑hearted ones.”
segments.s078-g90: به ویژه is an adverbial phrase meaning “especially.” After it ز introduces the source/cause (here “from” or “among”), and گردان مازنداران is a noun phrase: گردان “troop/host” followed by مازنداران, the people/lords of Mazandaran, specifying whose troop it is.
segments.s078-g91: بشد here is a literary past/imperfective marker meaning “it happened / he was (made)” and introduces the incident that follows: سام یکزخم = “Sam [was] one‑wounded” or “Sam received a single wound.” The conjunction و links the next coordinated clause بنشست زال, where بنشست is the simple past “sat down” with زال as subject.
segments.s078-g92: می‌و is an instrumental/adjunct noun (“with fruit”) placed before the verb to indicate what is used or supplied; مجلس is the direct object “the assembly/court.” آراست is the past transitive “adorned” (he adorned the assembly). The conjunction و introduces بفراخت یال, where بفراخت is a past verb “he flung up/raised” and یال is its object “mane,” i.e. “he spread/raised his mane.”
segments.s078-g17: نشست is a past tense verb here meaning 'alighted/dismounted' (نشستن can mean 'sit' or 'alight'); the phrase از بر functions as a compound prepositional idea 'from off (the) back of'. باره تیزرو is a poetic noun phrase where تیزرو (swift) qualifies باره; together they mean 'swift mount/charger'. Word order is typical Persian noun phrase: determiner-like verb + prepositional phrase indicating source.
segments.s078-g18: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. از کوه marks origin ('from the mountain'), سر برکشد is a verbal phrase 'lifts up its head' (سر here = summit/head, برکشد = present subjunctive/past-form poetic usage of بازکشیدن/برکشیدن meaning 'raise/lift'); ماه نو is the subject of that clause 'the new moon'. The sequence is the standard poetic order: temporal marker + origin + verb + subject phrase.
segments.s078-g19: یکی is an indefinite article 'a certain'/'one'. تاج زرین is a straightforward noun + adjective 'golden crown'. نگارش گهر is a poetic compound: نگارش functions here as an attributive noun meaning an ornamentation/decoration (literally 'workmanship/style') and گهر 'jewel(s)'; together they indicate the crown is set/ornamented with gems.
segments.s078-g20: نهاد is the past tense (3rd‑singular) of نهادن 'to place/put' meaning 'he placed'. از بر here again marks the locus 'upon/on' (poetic order 'نهاد از بر X' = 'placed upon X'). تارک زال زر is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the brow/head (târak) of Zal (the) gold' — poetically 'Zal's brow' — where تارک is the head/top and زال زر names/qualifies the person. The verb governs the following prepositional phrase indicating where the crown was placed.
segments.s078-g21: به کابل رسیدند is a complete verbal phrase: به + place + رسیدند (past, 3rd plural) 'they arrived at Kabul'. خندان و شاد are two participial/adjectival words placed after the verb to indicate manner/state 'smiling and happy'. The subject is implied ('they') and shown by the plural verb form.
segments.s078-g22: سخنهای دیرینه is the object noun phrase 'old/ancient tales' (سخنهای = 'words/tales', دیرینه = 'ancient/long‑standing'). کردند یاد is a light-verb construction where یاد کردن (here split as کردند + یاد by poetic word order) means 'they remembered' or 'they called to mind' — literally 'they made remembrance'.
segments.s078-g23: همه شهر is the subject 'the whole city'. ز + آوای هندی درای is a poetic causal/possessive phrase: ز (from/because of) + آوا (melody/voice) + هندی (Indian/Indian style) + درای (poetic variant of دارا/دارای 'having, rich in'). Together they mean 'the whole city, being rich in Indian melody' — درای functions as an adjectival/predicative 'having/possessing'.
segments.s078-g24: ز introduces the cause/source again 'from/by the lamenting (of)'. نالیدن here is a noun/masdar sense 'lamenting/keening' (the masdar form used as source). The instrument names بربط و چنگ و نای follow in a list; in poetry the ezafe is often elided so it reads literally 'from the lamenting (on) barbat, harp, and ney'.
segments.s078-g9: تو گفتی is a direct address in the past: تو = 'you' (subject) followed by گفتی 'you said'. مگر here is a rhetorical particle meaning 'perhaps / isn't it?' leading into a question about روز انجامش — روز (noun, 'day') + انجامش (انجام + ـش, 'its end/completion') with the copula است finishing the clause. Word order is familiar Persian: topic/phrase then copula.
segments.s078-g10: This gives two alternative identifications. یکی introduces one of the possibilities ('one [possibility]'), followed by رستخیز 'resurrection'. The fused form است‌گر is an archaic/poetic sequence of copula + گر (literally 'is — if' or 'or if'), here functioning to present the second alternative: 'or (if) [it] is'. رامش 'tranquility/rest' and the final است are the second nominal predicate with its copula. The clause therefore lists alternatives: 'either X is, or Y is.'
segments.s078-g11: همی is a continuous/aspectual particle ('kept on / was still'), so همی رفت is a habitual/past-continuous sense 'he kept going'. ازین گونه is a two-part expression 'from this manner / in this way' (از + ین as orthographic variant of این + گونه 'manner'), تا پیش سام 'until before/in the presence of Sam' (تا = until, پیش = 'in the presence of').
segments.s078-g12: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'he alighted / came down' (فرود = alighting/descending + آمد = came). از اسپ = 'from the steed/horse'. The conjunction و links the next action; بگذارد here is a classical-narrative present/subjunctive form used to narrate action ('he puts / set down') and means 'to place/put', and گام ('step'/'foot') is the object: 'and he set his foot down.'
segments.s078-g13: گرفتش is a past transitive verbal form built from گرفت + ـش (object pronoun 'him'): 'he seized/took him'. جهان‌پهلوان follows as the title/epithet (the agent 'the world‑champion'); Persian allows verb + object + agent order. در کنار is a locative phrase 'at/into the side' = 'at his side' (در = in/at, کنار = beside).
segments.s078-g14: بپرسیدش is a past narrative form meaning 'he asked him' (the b- prefix with the past stem is common in classical narration; ـش is the object 'him'). از introduces the topic 'about/concerning', گردش روزگار is a genitival phrase: گردش 'turning/circuit/course' + روزگار 'time/fate' — together 'the course (turnings) of fate / the course of events'.
segments.s078-g15: شه کابلستان is a title phrase 'the king of Kabulestan' (شه = king + Kabulستان = the land/region of Kabul). گرفت is the simple past 'he took/seized'. آفرین is an interjection here ('bravo' / 'well done') expressing praise or acclamation.
segments.s078-g16: This is an exclamatory clause. چه here functions as an exclamatory 'what/how' (introducing astonishment), بر سام و بر زال is 'upon Sam and upon Zal' (the preposition بر + proper names), زر 'gold' is the object/thing being exclaimed about, and همچنین 'likewise/also' attaches the exclamation to both men. Word order (what + on X and on Y + gold) is poetic/exclamatory.
segments.s078-g49: بفرمود is an honorific/perfect‑tense reportive verb ('he commanded/said') that introduces a subordinate clause marked by تا (here functioning as a subordinating 'that/so that'). Within the تا‑clause, رفت is the past tense verb 'went' and مهراب is its subject (a proper name). پیش is an adverbial particle meaning 'forward / in the presence of / before' modifying رفت ('went forward/approached').
segments.s078-g50: ببستند is a past plural transitive verb 'they bound/tied'. عقدی is the object, here best read as a ceremonial 'knot/necklace' (the direct thing bound). برآیین is an adverbial phrase 'according to custom/rite' qualifying the action, و is the coordinating 'and', and کیش here is paired with آیین to indicate 'creed/rite' or customary ritual.
segments.s078-g51: به is the preposition introducing the goal/place ('onto/at'), یک تختشان is the noun phrase 'one/the same throne' with the possessive suffix -شان ('their/them' or 'them on their throne'), شاد is an adverbial/adjective 'glad/joyful' modifying the action, and بنشاندند is the past plural causative/transitive verb 'they seated/installed (them)'. Word order is preposition + place phrase + manner + verb.
segments.s078-g52: This is a simple coordinated clause: عقیق و زبرجد are two direct objects 'agates and peridots' (names of gems) and برافشاندند is the past plural verb 'they scattered / showered', describing the ceremonial act of showering gems.
segments.s078-g53: سر here means 'head' (the physical head or 'headpiece'), ماه is used as an epithet 'the beautiful one'/'the moon' (a common poetic term for a beautiful person), با marks accompaniment 'with' (here 'with an afsar'), افسر is the headpiece/diadem/helmet, and the compound نام دار (نام + دار) yields 'renowned/famed'—so the whole segment describes 'the beauty's head with a famed afsar.' Word order is noun + possessive sense + accompaniment phrase.
segments.s078-g54: Parallel to the previous half-line: سر 'head', شاه 'the king' (possessor), با 'with', تاج 'crown', گوهرنگار an adjectival compound 'gem‑inscribed / jewel‑encrusted.' The line uses parataxis (parallel noun phrases) to contrast the two heads and their respective headgear; no finite verb appears because it's a poetic nominal clause.
segments.s078-g55: بیاورد is a past/preterite third‑person verb 'he brought' (or 'he produced'). پس functions as a temporal/sequence adverb 'then' (it links this action to what preceded). دفتر is the direct object 'book / volume,' and خواسته here functions as a noun/participle 'requested / the requests' (i.e., 'the book of requests' or 'the requested book').
segments.s078-g56: یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one / a certain' (here 'one copy'), نسخت is an older/poetic form meaning 'copy/folio' (presented as a noun), گنج is 'treasure' (meta‑description of the volume), and آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned / decorated.' The noun phrase apposes or characterizes the brought book: 'one copy, an adorned treasure.'
segments.s078-g73: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past verb 'they departed/ went'; in epic Persian the verb can appear early for poetic cadence. شادان دل is a compound adjective meaning 'joyful-hearted' (شادان modifies دل), and خوش‌منش is a second coordinated adjective linked by و describing the same subject ('gracious/courteous').
segments.s078-g74: پر از آفرین لب is an adjectival phrase where پر 'full (of)' takes از + آفرین ('praise, acclamation') to qualify لب 'lips' (i.e. 'lips full of praise'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/for' linking the praise to its reason, and نیکی کنش 'good deed(s)/action' explains what the praise is for; کنش is a noun 'action/deed'.
segments.s078-g75: رسیدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they arrived/reached'. پیروز is a predicate adjective 'victorious'. تا + نیمروز here expresses extent/place ('as far as / to Nimruz' — Nimruz as a region), so the phrase says they arrived at/ reached Nimruz victorious.
segments.s078-g76: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' qualifying the following string of coordinated adjectives: شاد 'happy', خندان 'smiling/cheerful', and the compound adjective گیتی فروز (gīti‑frouz) 'world‑brightening / glorious' — a poetic epithet. The adjectives are linked by و and describe the celebratory mood.
segments.s078-g77: یکی here means 'a certain' / 'at one (banquet)'; بزم 'banquet/feast' is the setting, سام is the agent. آنگهی 'then' sequences the action. ساز کرد literally 'made an instrument' but functions idiomatically as 'made music / struck up a tune' (ساز + کرد with کرد as the past auxiliary).
segments.s078-g78: سه روز is the temporal phrase 'three days'. اندران 'in/among it' refers back to that feast (i.e. during that time there). بگماز کرد is a compound action: بگماز (appoint/establish) + کرد (did) — here meaning he caused/arranged the feast to continue or be held for three days.
segments.s078-g79: پس آنگاه are sequential adverbs 'then/afterwards' introducing the next action. سیندخت is the subject (proper name), آنجا is the locative 'there', and بماند is the past form 'remained/was left' (3rd-person singular), so the clause means 'Sindokht remained there.'
segments.s078-g80: خود و لشکرش 'himself and his army' is a coordinated object phrase (لشکر + enclitic possessive -ش). سوی کابل is a prepositional phrase 'toward Kabul.' براند is a past narrative verb (with the prefixed ب‑ common in epic diction) meaning 'drove/led/moved' (3rd-person singular), so the clause says someone took himself and his army toward Kabul (the subject is omitted here and supplied from context).
segments.s078-g41: چنین ('thus') is an adverb placing the manner of the answer first. داد is the past tense of دادن ('gave/said') functioning here as the main verb 'gave/uttered' and precedes its object پاسخ ('answer/reply'). به سیندخت is a dative phrase 'to Sindokht'; سام (the subject) is placed after the phrase for poetic emphasis (inverted order).
segments.s078-g42: که introduces the content clause (that / '…'): ازمن ('from me') marks the source of the request, and بخواه is the 2nd-person imperative/present-subjunctive of خواستن ('ask/wish') meaning 'ask (of)'. آنچه is a relative pronoun 'that which / whatever'; آیدت is a verbal form 'comes to you' with the enclitic -ت 'to you' indicating the experiencer; کام ('desire') is the noun governed by آنچه — 'whatever comes to your desire.'
segments.s078-g43: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed' (بر- + رفتند). تا here has a directional/limit sense 'as far as / to' and links the verb to the goal خانه ('house'). زرنگار modifies خانه as a postpositive adjective meaning 'gilded / richly adorned.'
segments.s078-g44: کجا introduces a relative/adverbial clause 'where'. اندرو is an archaic fused locative meaning 'in it / within', followed by بود (past 'was') and the subject phrase خرم بهار ('joyful Spring'), which appears after the verb by the common poetic inversion — 'where within was the joyful Spring.'
segments.s078-g45: نگه کرد is a compound verb 'he looked / he gazed' (نگه + کرد). سام is the subject. اندران ('among them') locates the gaze, and ماه روی is a descriptive compound 'moon‑faced' (ماه 'moon' used figuratively + روی 'face') describing those he looked at.
segments.s078-g46: یکایک ('one by one, each and every') modifies شگفتی ('marvel/wonder'). بماند is a past/pluperfect-ish form meaning 'he remained / was left' (ب- prefix with ماندن) and اندروی ('inwardly / within') is an adverbial locution indicating his internal reaction: he remained inwardly struck by each wonder.
segments.s078-g47: ندانست is the simple past 'he did not know'. کش here is an archaic/obscure element and does not match the provided basic senses (it functions to mark the indirect interrogative/measure 'by what / how'), so its exact sense is irregular. چون ستاید همی = 'how (to) praise' — چون 'how' + ستاید (verb of praise) with همی a classical emphatic/continuous particle marking the action. Overall: 'he did not know by what way/how to praise.'
segments.s078-g48: The second coordinated clause gives the alternative: برو here is an archaic coordinating/expletive particle (not the ordinary imperative 'go') used to link the two possibilities, and so it does not match the simple verb sense. چشم را is the object 'his eyes' with را as the definite/object marker. چون گشاید همی = 'how to open' (چون 'how' + گشاید 'open' — classical form) with همی again marking the action; together: 'nor how to open his eyes.'
segments.s078-g65: سر ماه is a temporal phrase meaning "at the head/start of the month" (سر = start/top + ماه = month). سام نریمان is the subject (a personal name, Sam son of Nariman). برفت is a past-tense verb meaning "he went off/ set out" (poetic/archaic perfect; the prefix بر- is an older/emphatic element attached to رفت).
segments.s078-g66: سوی سیستان is a prepositional/ directional phrase "toward Sistan" (سوی = toward + سیستان = the place). روی بنهاد is a verb phrase where روی ("face") is the object/thing being placed and بنهاد (past of نهادن) means "he set/placed/turned" — together idiomatically "he turned/put his face toward". تفت is an obscure/archaic element here and functions at the end of the clause; its exact lexical meaning is uncertain in this context.
segments.s078-g67: This is a coordinted noun phrase listing the leader and his accompaniments: ابا زال is a personal name or vocative plus name (here the leader), followed by با + لشکر indicating accompaniment "with an army," and then additional coordinated instruments/forces (پیل "elephants", کوس "war‑drum"). The conjunction و links the items.
segments.s078-g68: This clause names the abstract agent/condition (زمانه, here meaning 'fortune' or 'the times'), then رکاب ("stirrup") as the direct object. ورا is the enclitic/threreferential pronoun meaning "to him/there," and داد is the past verb "gave." بوس ("kiss") here is a noun used with داد to mean "gave a kiss" — a formulaic way to say he kissed the stirrup or paid homage to it.
segments.s078-g69: This is a list of built items: عماری (built dwellings/buildings), و links the items, بالای here names upper rooms/chambers, and هودج is a palanquin/sedan. The final word بساخت is the past tense verb meaning "he built" (archaic/poetic form of ساختن).
segments.s078-g70: یکی مهد is "one/a certain cradle." تا introduces a purposive clause here meaning "so that / in order that," ماه را is the definite object "the moon" (here an epithet for a beautiful woman), در is the locative "in/within," and نشاخت is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning something like "he placed/laid" (the precise lexical reading is archaic).
segments.s078-g71: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." The following nouns are coordinated: سیندخت (a woman's name), و linking, مهراب (a name here), و linking, پیوند خویش where پیوند (literally 'bond' / 'ally') plus خوی ("kinship") with the enclitic ش = "her" yields the sense "her kinsman/ally" or "her spouse/relative." Grammatically, the phrase lists the people who act in the main clause.
segments.s078-g72: سوی سیستان is the directional phrase "toward Sistan." روی کردند is a compound where روی ("face") is the noun used in an idiom with کردن to mean "they turned (their) faces," and کردند is the past tense 3rd‑person plural of کردن. پیش functions adverbially as "forward/onto/before" emphasizing movement toward the place.
segments.s078-g57: برو here functions as a narrative preverb/verb meaning 'went (and)...' that combines with the following past verb خواند to yield 'went and read' (a common classical Persian sequence). از گنجها is a prepositional phrase 'from the treasures', and هر چه بود is a relative/duration phrase 'whatever there was' where هر چه = 'whatever/anything that' and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s078-g58: که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. گوش آن = 'that ear' (or 'its ear') with آن pointing back to the listener(s). نیارست is an older/polite negative-past sense meaning 'could not / did not endure' (the exact lexical senses given do not list this reading). گفتی functions as a noun 'what was said / the saying', and شنود is archaic present/subjunctive-form of 'to hear' used here in sense 'to hear' (i.e. 'could not hear / could not bear to hear').
segments.s078-g59: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/went away'. از آنجا is the locative prepositional phrase 'from there'. به جای نشست literally 'sat to/in their place' — به جای indicates the place/destination and نشست is the past 'sat' (they sat down or settled).
segments.s078-g60: ببودند is a past plural 'they were/they remained' (classical form of بود). یک هفته is a duration phrase 'for one week'. با + noun indicates accompaniment or instrument; با می‌به دست = 'with mey‑be in hand' (idiomatically: drinking/holding the wine‑drink).
segments.s078-g61: وز here is an archaic/formal variant of 'از' meaning 'from' (classical usage), so وز ایوان = 'from the iwan'. سوی باغ رفتند = 'they went toward the garden' with سوی marking direction and رفتند the past plural 'they went'. باز is a postpositive adverb 'again'.
segments.s078-g62: سه هفته is a duration 'three weeks'. به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'in joy/for rejoicing'. گرفتند is past plural 'they seized/took up' and here governs ساز (musical instrument), so گرفتند ساز = 'they took up/played instruments' or more idiomatically 'they made music'.
segments.s078-g63: بزرگان کشورش is a genitival phrase 'the great ones/nobles of his country' — کشور + suffix ش marks 'his realm'. با دست بند is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with hand-fetters' (دست بند = fetter/handcuff); با marks the manner/means.
segments.s078-g64: کشیدند is the past plural 'they drew/they dragged'. بر پیش together mark direction/location 'before/in front of' (بر + پیش), and کاخ بلند is the noun phrase 'the lofty/tall palace' as the place toward which the dragging is done. The direct object (the person dragged) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s078-g33: همه (subject) + سام را (Sam marked with را for definite/emphatic object) + آفرین خواندند is a fixed clause meaning 'they shouted/praised “Bravo!”'. خواندند is past 3rd plural; آفرین here is an interjection/noun used as the thing they 'read/sang/called'. Word order (subject — object — predicate) is normal for classical Persian emphasis.
segments.s078-g34: پس = 'then' (temporal connector). از جام گوهر functions as a prepositional phrase 'from the cup (came) jewels' where گوهر is the thing coming out of the cup. برافشاندند is past 3rd plural of برافشاندن 'to scatter/sprinkle/ shower (down)'.
segments.s078-g35: بدان جشن = به + آن (classical fusion) + جشن, i.e. 'in/at that feast'. هر کس که آمد = 'every person who came' (که introduces the relative clause). فراز here is adverbial, 'forth/forward/up', meaning 'came forward' or 'rose up'. Note the somewhat archaic fused preposition (بدان) and the relative clause structure.
segments.s078-g36: شد = 'became' (past 3rd sing. agreeing with the indefinite 'هر کس'). از خواسته = 'from (their) desire/want' (خواسته as noun). یک به یک = adverbial phrase 'one by one' (به used here as 'by/—ly'). بی‌نیاز is the predicate adjective 'free from need/needless', completing 'became free from desire'.
segments.s078-g37: بخندید is past 3rd plural 'they laughed'. و links the action to the following speech. سیندخت را سام گفت is an inverted order common in classical Persian: 'Sam said (addressing) Sindokht' — سیندخت را (Sindokht marked with را for the addressee/emphatic object), سام is the speaker, and گفت is the past of 'say'.
segments.s078-g38: که introduces the reported question/utterance. رودابه را = 'Rudāba' marked with را (direct object / person concerned). چند = interrogative 'how long/for how much'. خواهی is 2nd-person present/future of خواستن used here as a periphrastic future/auxiliary ('will you.../do you wish to...'). نهفت is the verb 'to hide/conceal' in an archaic form — together 'چند خواهی نهفت' = 'how long will you hide (her)?'.
segments.s078-g39: بدو = به + او 'to him' (dative). گفت = 'said' (past). سیندخت is the speaker (subject). هدیه کجاست is a direct interrogative clause 'where is the gift?' with کجا + ست functioning as 'where is'. The word order (dative — verb — subject — question) is typical of older poetic prose.
segments.s078-g40: اگر = 'if'. دیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the seeing' or 'to see'. آفتابت is 'your sun' (آفتاب + second-person suffix) used metaphorically for a beloved/bright one. هواست = هوا + است, where هوا means 'desire/wish' here and است is the copula — 'if seeing your sun is (your) desire/wish.'
segments.s078-g81: سپرد is a past transitive verb 'entrusted/handed over' with an implicit subject ('he'); آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' (آن = then + زمان = time) placed after the verb for poetic word order. پادشاهی is the direct object 'the kingship' and به + زال marks the recipient ('to Zal') — the pattern سپرد ... به X = 'entrusted ... to X'.
segments.s078-g82: برون برد is a two-part expression meaning 'led out / brought forth' where برون (forth/out) is adverbial and برد is the action verb; poetic inversion places برون before برد. لشکر is the object 'army/host', and به فرخنده فال is a prepositional phrase of manner 'with/under an auspicious omen' (فرخنده = auspicious, فال = omen).
segments.s078-g83: سوی + noun is a directional phrase 'toward(s)'; گرگساران ('wolf‑lands' / 'wolf‑people') names the place or peoples approached. شد is the simple past/intransitive 'he went/turned' here (poetic use), and باختر follows as an adverbial/directional 'westward' — compact poetic ordering where the motion and the direction are coordinated by و.
segments.s078-g84: درفش خجسته is a noun phrase 'the auspicious banner/standard' (درفش = banner, خجسته = auspicious). برافراخت is a past transitive verb 'he hoisted/raised', and سر here is used adverbially ('on high', literally 'head/top') — the fixed poetic collocation برافراخت سر = 'raised aloft'.
segments.s078-g85: شوم here qualifies گفت and means 'ill‑omened/ominous' or 'with ill‑omen' (an adverbial/epithet attached to 'said'). کان is a demonstrative 'that' introducing the subordinate clause, پادشاهی is 'kingship', and مراست is an archaic fused expression meaning 'is mine' (مرا + است compressed) — an old possessive copula; the clause thus reads 'that kingship is mine.'
segments.s078-g86: دل و دیده is a parallel nominal phrase 'heart and eye' (metaphors for loyalty/attention). با ما ندارند is a present‑tense negative predicate 'are not with us' (با = with, ما = us, ندارند = they do not have / are not possessed of/are not aligned), and راست at the end is an emphatic particle 'truly' / 'indeed' confirming the statement.
segments.s078-g87: منوچهر is the agent (proper name). منشور آن شهر is the noun phrase 'the charter of that city' (منشور = charter/tablet, آن شهر = that city). بر here begins the dative/benefactive construction that links into the next phrase (classical order: X بر Y داد = 'placed X on/over Y' / 'gave X to Y').
segments.s078-g88: مرا داد is the core 'gave to me' (مرا = 'me' + را object marker, داد = 'gave'); note the object marker ra attaches to the pronoun. گفتا is 'he said' (past), and the following phrase همی دار و خوار is a terse reported command: همی (always/continually), دار here functions as an archaic imperative 'hold/keep' (imperative form), and خوار is an adjective 'low/despised' used as a verbal injunction (roughly 'abase them' or 'hold in contempt'). The exact nuance is idiomatic and archaic in Ferdowsi's language.
segments.s078-g25: تو (you) is the 2nd-person address; گفتی is the simple past 2nd-person singular of گفتن (“you said”) and introduces reported speech without an explicit complementizer (a common poetic device). دد و دام is a coordinated noun phrase (“hostility/harshness and trap”), and رامشگرست is رامشگر + است (contracted to -ست) — a predicate nominal in 3rd-person singular meaning “is a peacemaker/soother.” The structure is: ‘You said (that) [X is Y]’ with the content clause following the reporting verb directly.
segments.s078-g26: زمانه is the subject meaning “the time / the age.” به آرایشی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase where به marks manner/guise (“in/with an arrangement/array”), and آرایشی (آرایش + ی) denotes an arrangement/array. دیگرست is دیگر + است (contracted) meaning “is different/other” in 3rd-person singular. Together: ‘the age, in an arrangement/guise, is other,’ i.e. ‘the times are set in a different array.’
segments.s078-g27: This is a descriptive noun phrase with an omitted ezāfe (typical of classical Persian): یال اسپان = ‘the manes of the horses.’ The sequence بش و یال اسپان lists two horse-parts (the first item is archaic/uncertain here), then کران تا کران is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘from edge to edge / from rim to rim.’ Word order places the noun phrase followed by the extent phrase (کران تا کران).
segments.s078-g28: بر اندوده is a compound verbal/adjectival phrase meaning ‘smeared upon / coated over’ (بر + اندوده). پر here functions as an adjectival premodifier meaning ‘full (of)’, so پر مشک و پر زعفران = ‘full of musk and full of saffron.’ Note the poetic/prepositional ز (from/of) after پر to link the substance. The whole phrase describes the coating applied to the horses’ manes/flanks.
segments.s078-g29: برون‌رفت is a noun meaning ‘coming forth / appearance.’ It is followed by the proper name سیندخت; classical Persian often omits the ezāfe, so this reads ‘the coming forth of Sindokht’ or ‘Sindokht’s coming forth.’ با بندگان is a prepositional phrase ‘with servants/attendants.’
segments.s078-g30: میان بسته is a participial/adjectival cluster meaning ‘bound/enclosed in the midst’ (میان = ‘among/in the middle’, بسته = past participle ‘bound/tied’). It modifies the following noun phrase سیصد پرستندگان (‘three hundred worshippers’), with the numeral preceding the noun, as is normal in Persian poetic order.
segments.s078-g31: This line uses an archaic/dense word order. مر is an old dative/forative particle (functioning like ‘to/for’ in this context) introducing the beneficiary; آن here is the demonstrative ‘those/that,’ and هر یکی را is ‘each one’ with را marking the definite/direct object of the implied giving. یکی جام زر is the gift ‘one golden cup.’ The verb ‘gave’ is omitted (a typical poetic ellipsis); the phrase thus reads ‘to each of those, one golden cup (was given).’
segments.s078-g32: به دست اندرون is a locative/instrumental phrase meaning literally ‘to the inner hand’ or ‘into the hand within’ (به marking direction/recipient, دست = hand, اندرون = inside/inner). پر ز مشک و گهر is a descriptive phrase ‘full of musk and gem’ (پر = full (of), ز = poetic ‘of/from’, مشک = musk, گهر = jewel). Together they qualify how/where the cup was placed: ‘into their inner hand, full of musk and jewel.’

sentences.s078-s1: He blew the mihrab’s ney and sounded the drum; the host was arrayed like a rooster’s eye.
sentences.s078-s2: With tattered elephants and peacemakers, the earth became a paradise from edge to edge.
sentences.s078-s3: Because of so many variegated silken banners—whether red and white or yellow and purple.
sentences.s078-s4: What music of flute and what music of harp — the roar of trumpets and the ringing of bells.
sentences.s078-s45: I fear the tumult of evil‑hearted ones, especially the troop of the Mazandaranis.
sentences.s078-s46: Sam received a single wound, and Zal sat down; he adorned the assembly with fruit and spread his mane.
sentences.s078-s9: He dismounted from his swift steed, as when the new moon lifts its head from the mountain.
sentences.s078-s10: He set a golden crown, studded with jewels, upon Zal's brow.
sentences.s078-s11: They reached Kabul laughing and joyful, and they recalled old tales.
sentences.s078-s12: The whole city, rich in Indian melody, resounded with the lamenting of the barbat, the harp, and the ney.
sentences.s078-s5: You said, 'Is that perhaps the day of its ending? Is it a resurrection, or is it repose?'
sentences.s078-s6: He continued on in that way until before Sam; he dismounted from his steed and placed his foot down.
sentences.s078-s7: The world‑champion seized him and set him at his side; he asked him about the course of events.
sentences.s078-s8: The king of Kabulestan took it — 'Bravo!' — and what gold likewise upon Sam and upon Zal!
sentences.s078-s25: He commanded that Mehrab go forward; they bound a ceremonial knot according to rite and custom.
sentences.s078-s26: They joyfully seated them upon one throne, and scattered agates and peridots.
sentences.s078-s27: On the beauty's head a famed afsar (diadem), on the king's head a gem‑studded crown.
sentences.s078-s28: Then he brought the requested book — one copy, a treasure adorned.
sentences.s078-s37: They departed, joyful at heart and gracious in manner, their lips full of praise for good deeds.
sentences.s078-s38: They reached Nimruz victorious, so joyful and smiling, and world-brightening.
sentences.s078-s39: At one banquet Sam then made music; he kept that feast going for three days.
sentences.s078-s40: Then Sindokht remained there; he (or someone) took himself and his army toward Kabul.
sentences.s078-s21: Thus Sam answered Sindokht: "Ask of me whatever your desire may be."
sentences.s078-s22: They went on to the gilded house, where within the joyful Spring was.
sentences.s078-s23: Sam gazed at the moon‑faced among them; at each marvel he remained inwardly (astonished).
sentences.s078-s24: He did not know how to praise (her), nor how to open his eyes (to her).
sentences.s078-s33: At the beginning of the month Sam, son of Nariman, set out and turned his face toward Sistan.
sentences.s078-s34: Aba Zāl, with an army, elephants and war‑drums; fortune put the stirrup before him — he gave it a kiss.
sentences.s078-s35: He built dwellings, upper chambers and a sedan; he made a single cradle so that the (fair) moon might be placed within it.
sentences.s078-s36: When Sindokht and Mehrab and her kin-ally turned, they directed their faces toward Sistan.
sentences.s078-s29: He went and read from the treasures whatever there was that the ear could not bear to hear.
sentences.s078-s30: They departed from there and sat in their place; they remained for a week with mey-be (a poetic wine‑drink) in hand.
sentences.s078-s31: From the iwan they went again toward the garden; for three weeks they made music in celebration.
sentences.s078-s32: The great men of his realm, with their hands fettered, dragged (him) before the lofty palace.
sentences.s078-s17: Everyone cried “Bravo!” to Sām; then they showered jewels from the cup.
sentences.s078-s18: At that feast, every person who came forward became, one by one, free from want.
sentences.s078-s19: They laughed, and Sam said to Sindokht, 'How long will you keep Rudāba hidden?'
sentences.s078-s20: Sindokht said to him, 'Where is the gift, if seeing your sun is your desire?'
sentences.s078-s41: At that time he entrusted the kingship to Zal and led the army forth under an auspicious omen.
sentences.s078-s42: He moved toward the wolf‑lands, and westward he hoisted the auspicious standard on high.
sentences.s078-s43: He said ominously, 'That kingship is mine; their heart and eye are not with us, truly.'
sentences.s078-s44: Manūchehr put the charter of that city in my charge, gave it to me, and said, 'Keep it always — abase (them).'
sentences.s078-s13: You said that cruelty and traps are peacemakers; the times are fashioned in another guise.
sentences.s078-s14: The horses’ flanks and manes, from edge to edge, were smeared over — full of musk and full of saffron.
sentences.s078-s15: Sindokht came forth with her attendants, having enclosed three hundred worshippers in the midst.
sentences.s078-s16: To each of them he gave one golden cup, into the inner hand, full of musk and jewel.
